x_posts.source_theme: status quo as the real competitor
x_posts.text:
The owner you're pitching isn't comparing you to another manager. They're comparing you to doing nothing.

Most operators never figure that out.
Self-managing owners aren't irrational. They're overwhelmed. The pitch that adds cognitive load to that state is working against itself.

Most stalled VRM deals break down at the change decision, not the selection decision.

The owner isn't choosing between you and a competitor. They haven't decided to change anything yet. Running a better pitch at that problem doesn't fix it.

The timing of your outreach matters more than the quality of your pitch.

An owner who just handled a 2 AM maintenance call alone is not the same buyer they were six months ago. Most operators have no system for knowing when that window opens.

A revenue projection is not an insight. It's a number the owner could have run themselves on AirDNA.

The operator who walks in with a specific observation about this property, this listing, this owner's particular situation, is in a different conversation.

Two operators pitch the same owner.

One brings a revenue projection. The other walks in and says: your cleanliness score is a 4.2, every comparable above 4.8 commands 18% more per night, and you have a $9,000 gap a cleaning protocol change can close.

Same market. Same owner. One gets the listing.

Owners who come in through a trigger event, a bad review streak, a regulation change, a neighbor switching to professional management, convert at higher rates than owners who come through general outreach.

What trigger events are you watching for in your market right now?

Went deeper on Jill Konrath's SNAP Selling in this week's VRM Sales Library newsletter: stalled deal diagnostic, scripts for each decision stage, and the owner-type breakdown for the insight approach. Link in bio.

Been applying @jillkonrath's SNAP Selling to VRM owner acquisition this week. The framing that changed how I think about it: the real competitor in most listing appointments isn't another management company. It's the owner's status quo. The change decision is where deals actually break down.

threads.source_theme: SNAP framework applied to VRM pipeline stages
tweets: Most VRM owner acquisition pitches lose to the wrong competitor.
The self-managing owner you're pitching isn't comparing you to another manager. They're comparing you to doing nothing. That's a different problem.

Jill Konrath's SNAP Selling maps to every stage of VRM owner acquisition: how you earn the first conversation, how you move the owner past inertia, and how you differentiate when they finally get to comparison mode.

The three decisions every owner makes before signing: access (will they give you time), change (will they actually consider switching), selection (which operator do they choose). Most operators only work on the third one.

The insight that lands: before your next listing appointment, find one specific thing about that owner's property that they don't know. Not a revenue projection. An observation tied to their actual listing data that changes how they see their own situation.
That's the difference between a vendor with a spreadsheet and an operator who understands the asset.
